data.body:
## Context
When a developer starting a new government digital service project runs /dso:onboarding and project-detect.sh returns no recognized framework, there's no guided path to get started — they must manually set up their application stack before onboarding can detect and configure anything. Nava PBC maintains 4 vetted application templates (NextJS, Flask, Rails, Jekyll USWDS) that provide production-ready starting points with CI, Docker, and testing infrastructure, but there's no integration between these templates and the DSO onboarding flow.

## Success Criteria
1. When /dso:onboarding runs project-detect.sh and the returned framework type is empty or unknown, it offers a template selection menu before entering Phase 2. Users who decline proceed to the existing manual flow unchanged.
2. A template registry config file (shipped with DSO plugin, version-controlled) defines available templates with metadata: name, repo URL (full HTTPS), install method (nava-platform or git-clone), framework type, and required --data flags. Missing/malformed -> fallback with warning. Registry parser validates required fields and allowlists install_method values — never shell-sources the file.
3. For nava-platform templates: probe uv/pipx, install CLI, verify --help exits 0, run app install with properly quoted --data flags from registry. Any failure -> clear error + manual fallback.
4. Jekyll USWDS: git clone with non-empty dir warning/abort, exit code checks, clear error on failure.
5. Post-install: re-run Phase 1 auto-detection, skip Phase 2, proceed to Phase 3. Config inferred from registry metadata + detection output. Registry framework_type values must match project-detect.sh output tokens.
6. After install + Phase 3, validate.sh --ci passes on the resulting project.

## Approach
Template Registry + Onboarding Gate. Config-driven template list with metadata. Auto-detect empty project -> offer menu -> install via nava-platform CLI or git clone -> re-detect -> skip to Phase 3.

## Scenario Analysis (20 surviving scenarios across 2 rounds)

Round 1: nava-platform install hangs (high), partial file writes (high), git clone non-empty dir (high), not on PATH (high), interactive prompts (high), Python <3.11 (medium), git <2.27 (medium), missing registry (high), missing registry fields (medium), path traversal (critical).

Round 2: URL redirect to auth wall (high), first-time CLI wizard (high), shell-source code execution (critical), --data quoting (high), validate.sh config collision (medium), HEAD non-reproducible installs (high), Docker read-only fs (medium), install_method allowlist (critical), unknown key masking (medium), framework type mismatch (high).

## Prerequisites (blocking)
Spike: validate headless nava-platform app install per template (CI, no TTY, exit 0). Test omitted --data -> non-zero exit.

## Post-Release Acceptance (non-gating)
Dogfooding: contributor onboards with each template, documents time-to-first-commit vs manual.

## Dependencies
nava-platform CLI (external, Nava PBC) — degrades gracefully. Spike validates before implementation.

## Research Findings
- nava-platform installed from GitHub via uv/pipx (not PyPI). --data KEY=VALUE suppresses copier prompts. No --defaults flag. Spike required.
- copier pinned to specific non-release commit in nava-platform. Post-install smoke test essential.

### Planning Intelligence Log
- Web research (Step 2.6): triggered (external integration, unfamiliar dependency)
- Scenario analysis (Step 2.75): re-triggered via gate. 20 scenarios surviving blue team.
- Practitioner-requested additional cycles: scenario analysis re-run 1 time
